coredata.dc:title: A novel carbon quantum dots-enhanced chemiluminescence method for the sensitive determination of iodide ion
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yamei
Wang, Xiaowei
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, P and Cl doped carbon dots (P/Cl-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. An obvious chemiluminescence (CL) signal was obtained when the P/Cl-CDs coexisted with acidic KMnO4, and then a stronger CL signal appeared when NaHSO3 was added into the mixed solution of KMnO4 and P/Cl-CDs. The CL property and mechanism of P/Cl-CDs in the presence of KMnO4 or/and NaHSO3 have been elucidated. It was found that iodide ion (I−) could strongly inhibit the CL signal of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system, and the degree of inhibition was proportional with the concentration of I−. The finding was used to design a method for the determination of I−. Under the optimum conditions, the CL signal of P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system decreased linearly with the logarithm of I− concentration in a range of 0.1–70.0 μM, and the detection limit was 90.0 nM. The method was applied to the determination of I− in edible salt samples. Conceivably, this novel CL system paves the way to numerous new assays based on the use of heteroatoms doping carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: P, Cl doped carbon dots
Chemiluminescence
Iodide ion
Quenching

Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province ( 2013011013–3 ), and Fundamental Research Fund of Modern College of Humanities and Sciences of Shanxi Normal University ( 2020JCYJ01 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI-CL system. (a) NaHSO3, (b) P/Cl-CDs, (c) water carrier, (d) I− standard or sample solution and (e) KMnO4 solution.

(a) TEM image (Inset: size distribution and HRTEM image). (b) XRD. (c) FL. (d) UV–vis absorption. (e) FT-IR spectrum. (f) XPS spectrum. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (g), Cl 2p (h) and P 2p (i) of P/Cl-CDs.

(a) CL kinetic curves of (1) P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4, (2) P/Cl-free CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 (3) P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3-I− and (4) P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3. (b) CL of reagent mixing orders of (1) P/Cl-CDs injection into KMnO4–NaHSO3, (2) NaHSO3 injection into P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 and (3) KMnO4 injection into P/Cl-CDs-NaHSO3. (c) CL spectra of P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 (1), P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3-I− (2) and P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 (3). (d) Effect of free radicals on P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system.

Effects of the concentration of (a) KMnO4, (b) NaHSO3 and (c) P/Cl-CDs on the CL intensity of P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system. (d) Calibration graphs of I−, (e) typical CL responses of I− solutions with different concentrations 1: 0.0 μM, 2: 0.1 μM, 3: 0.3 μM, 4: 0.7 μM, 5: 7.0 μM, 6: 30.0 μM, 7: 50.0 μM, 8: 70.0 μM.

CL intensity of P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system (black), P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3-I− (red) and P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3-I− with other species (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of the proposed iodide detection method with other methods.
Determination of I− in salt samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three measurements ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A novel carbon quantum dots-enhanced chemiluminescence method for the sensitive determination of iodide ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041000, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, P and Cl doped carbon dots (P/Cl-CDs) were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. An obvious chemiluminescence (CL) signal was obtained when the P/Cl-CDs coexisted with acidic KMnO4, and then a stronger CL signal appeared when NaHSO3 was added into the mixed solution of KMnO4 and P/Cl-CDs. The CL property and mechanism of P/Cl-CDs in the presence of KMnO4 or/and NaHSO3 have been elucidated. It was found that iodide ion (I−) could strongly inhibit the CL signal of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system, and the degree of inhibition was proportional with the concentration of I−. The finding was used to design a method for the determination of I−. Under the optimum conditions, the CL signal of P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system decreased linearly with the logarithm of I− concentration in a range of 0.1–70.0 μM, and the detection limit was 90.0 nM. The method was applied to the determination of I− in edible salt samples. Conceivably, this novel CL system paves the way to numerous new assays based on the use of heteroatoms doping carbon dots.

The CL signal was obtained from the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 system, which was significantly enhanced when NaHSO3 was added into the above system. The addition of I− resulted in significant CL quenching of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 CL system. The finding was used to design a method for the determination of I−.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) emission, whether in the ultraviolet, visible, or infrared range, is usually attributed to the transfer of chemical excitation energy to a molecule that does not undergo oxidation itself and then loses the excitation energy by emitting light of the correct wavelength [1]. The CL method is an attractive subject for intensive research because of its simple and inexpensive equipment and extremely low background signal. However, relatively weak CL intensity produced from some reactions, such as periodate-hydrogen peroxide reaction [2–4], Fenton reaction [5], sulfite-hydrogen peroxide reaction [6], and nitrite with acidified hydrogen peroxide reaction [7], presents some problems in developing sensitive analytical method, therefore, the improvement of CL systems for various analytical application is still a challenge. In recent years, novel CL systems and signal amplification techniques using nanoparticles have become an increasing focus of interest and have promote its application in various fields because of their important contribution to the excellent sensitivity and stability of CL method [8–13].

Carbon dots (CDs), a rising star among carbon-based nanomaterials, have attracted considerable attention since they were first produced in 2004 [14]. Rich synthesis methods and raw material sources entrust CDs with a great difference in size, structure and physicochemical property [15]. Nowadays, CDs are also widely used for the development of CL-based assays due to their outstanding chemical and optical properties [16], especially its fluorescence (FL) characteristics. CDs can participate in various CL reactions as emitting species, energy receptors or catalysts [2,5,17]. With these novel influences in CL reaction, these CD-related CL systems have been applied in a variety of fields, such as sensors, photodynamic therapy and bioimaging [7,17,18]. The CL properties of CDs depend on the complex interplay between the carbon core, functional groups, and doped heteroatoms [15]. Due to the small specific surface area, lack of functional surface groups, and low quantum efficiency, further applications of bare CDs in CL are limited [19]. Recent studies show that chemical doping of bare CDs with heteroatoms is one of the most efficient ways to solve these problems [20,21]. Doping with one or more heteroatoms changes the optical properties of the CDs, including ultraviolet visible absorption (UV–vis) and FL, and improves the quantum yield of the CDs and enriches the surface functional groups of the CDs. The doped CDs can be prepared from abundant raw materials by simple synthesis methods [22] and can be rationally designed to meet different requirements. This also arouses great interest in the development of CDs-based CL methods and greatly expands the application range of CL [23–26].

It is well known that iodide (I−) is an essential trace element for human body and plays an important role in thyroid function. Excessive or insufficient of I− in the body can lead some thyroid diseases such as hypothyroidism, goiter and hyperthyroidism, which are widespread public health problems in some developing countries [27]. Therefore, the development of simple and convenient determination methods for I− is of great importance. At present, the major methods for the detection of I− included colorimetry [28,29], FL [30–32], CL [33,34] and electrochemical detection [35–37].

In this work, CDs doped with elements P and Cl (P/Cl-CDs) were prepared using sucrose as the main precursor. It was found that KMnO4 could oxidize P/Cl-CDs to produce CL by injecting holes into P/Cl-CDs, which increased the holes in P/Cl-CDs and accelerated electron-hole annihilation, resulting in energy release in the form of CL emission. The CL intensity of P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 system was further enhanced by the addition of NaHSO3 because the excited state SO2 (SO2*), which is formed by the interaction between KMnO4 and NaHSO3, can transfer its energy to P/Cl-CDs and further enhance the CL emission. Moreover, the addition of I− resulted in significant CL quenching of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 CL system. On this basis, a simple, selective and sensitive CL sensing approach for I− was developed.

An IFIS-C flow injection (FI) system (Xi'an Remex Analysis Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China) was used to deliver the solutions. The CL signal was collected with a BPCL ultraweak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China) and then recorded using a computer with BPCL software. FL measurements were performed on a Cary Eclipse FL spectrophotometer (Cary, USA). UV–vis spectrum was obtained using a Cary 5000 spectrophotometer (Cary, USA). P/Cl-CDs were characterized with a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was analyzed with a Varian 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) study was collected by using Ultima IV-185 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected by a K-alpha surface analysis system (Thermo, USA).

All reagents in the experiments were analytical reagents. 30% H2O2, KMnO4, sucrose, KI and NaHSO3 were supplied by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). HCl and H3PO4 were purchased from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Factory (Luoyang, China).

The precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 0.5000 g sucrose, 0.15 mL HCl and 0.15 mL H3PO4 in 20 mL water. A series of P/Cl-CDs samples were prepared by controlling the temperature of the hydrothermal treatment and the elapsed reaction time. P/Cl-CDs were prepared at a specific temperature and a specific time, e.g., 180 °C, 6 h. The resulting product was transferred to the dark yellow vial. The solution was stable at 4 °C for several months. The P/Cl-free CDs were prepared according to the above method, except that HCl and H3PO4 were not added.

The schematic diagram in Fig. 1 illustrated the FI-CL detection system used in this work. Two peristaltic pumps and a six-way valve were connected by a Teflon tube. All flow tubes transported the reaction solution into the flow cell. The chemical reaction signal was converted and amplified by a photomultiplier tube (PMT, operated at −600 V) on a CL instrument, and a computer was used to collect the CL signal. The KMnO4 solution, I− standard solution or sample and carrier water were pumped into the flow cell at a rate of 2.5 mL min−1 by the peristaltic pump 2. The solutions of NaHSO3 and P/Cl-CDs were pumped into a six-way injection valve and then transported into the flow cell, an 8 cm sampling loop. The light output of the CL response was detected by a PMT and then recorded by a computer with a data acquisition interface. The net CL intensity ΔI = I 0−I s was used for the quantitative determination of I−, where I s and I 0 were the CL intensity in the presence and absence of I−, respectively.

The samples of iodized salt and non-iodized salt used in this study were purchased from the local supermarket. Six crucibles were used, three of which were filled with 0.5 g iodized salt samples and 2.0 mL 6.0 mol/L NaOH, and the other three were filled with 0.5 g non-iodized salt samples and 2.0 mL 6.0 mol/L NaOH. Six crucibles were placed in a high temperature furnace and fired at 500 °C for 1 h. After cooling, the residue in each crucible was soaked with 20 mL of deionized water and filtered, respectively. The filtrate was diluted to 50 mL with water and used for the determination of I−.

P/Cl-CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. The TEM image of P/Cl-CDs was used to determine their size distribution and morphology, which indicated they were spherical nanoparticles with size distribution of about 2.6 nm (Fig. 2 a). The particle size distribution, shown in inset of Fig. 2a, revealed that the prepared P/Cl-CDs had a lattice parameter of 0.22 nm, which was similar to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon [38]. The XRD patterns of P/Cl-CDs showed low intensity reflection centered at about 24° (Fig. 2b), which was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms [39]. One of the most popular characterizations of CDs is their FL properties, which usually depend on the excitation wavelength. The excitation wavelength dependent behavior was also observed for the freshly prepared P/Cl-CDs. There were two FL emission peaks at 460 nm and 560 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2c), and the first one exhibited a clear red shift, while the red shift of the second one was not obvious when the excitation wavelength changed. The UV–vis spectrum of P/Cl-CDs (Fig. 2d) showed two characteristic absorption peaks at 250 and 350 nm, related to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of P/Cl-CDs was also studied (Fig. 2e). The result indicated that there were some functional groups such as O–H, C–H CO, CC [40], C–P, and C–Cl in the surface of P/Cl-CDs, whose assigned peaks can be observed at 3400, 2927, 1435, 1374, 1040 and 842 cm−1. Moreover, the XPS spectrum of P/Cl-CDs confirmed the existence of P and Cl atoms, respectively (Fig. 2f). The C 1s spectrum in XPS showed four peaks at 288.5, 287.4, 286.1 and 284.6 eV, which were assigned to CO, C–Cl, C–O, and CC bonds, respectively (Fig. 2g). The existence of P–C and P–O was demonstrated in the high-resolution P 2p spectrum (Fig. 2h), with two peaks at 133.4 and 132.4 eV [41,42]. Two peaks at 199.8 and 198.1 eV (Fig. 2i) could be assigned to the Cl 2p 1/2 and Cl 2p 3/2 bonding [43]. These results were clear evidence that the P and Cl atoms were successfully doped.

The kinetic CL profiles of several CL systems were presented in Fig. 3 a. The reaction between P/Cl-CDs and KMnO4 gave rise to weak CL emission. The addition of NaHSO3 resulted in enhanced CL emission. Meanwhile, the P/Cl-free CDs were also added into the KMnO4–NaHSO3 system, the results showed that the CL signal was slightly higher than for the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 system and much lower than for the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system. This may be that the doping of P and Cl forms more active sites on the surface of P/Cl-CDs, which were suitable for this CL system. It was found that I− could quench the CL signal of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system. Thus, the developed CL system with a high emission intensity could be useful for analytical purposes. Almost no CL signal was observed in the P/Cl-CDs-NaHSO3 system or in the KMnO4–NaHSO3 system. The effect of sucrose, the used precursor of P/Cl-CDs synthesis, on the KMnO4–NaHSO3 system was also studied. The results showed that no noticeable CL was detected, suggesting that the enhancing effect was attributable to the synthesized P/Cl-CDs.
Different mixing orders of the reagents were investigated, and the largest signal was observed when KMnO4 was finally injected into the mixture of P/Cl-CDs-NaHSO3 (Fig. 3b).

The CL spectra of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 system and the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system were recorded in the absence or presence of I− (Fig. 3c). All three systems had an emission maximum at 535 nm, which was similar to the FL spectrum of P/Cl-CDs. This showed that the addition of NaHSO3 and/or I− only affected the CL intensity but did not change the nature of the luminophore. Therefore, the CL could be assigned to the excited states of P/Cl-CDs (P/Cl-CDs*), which was attributed to the radiative recombination of holes and electrons injected by oxidant in P/Cl-CDs [44]. It was noteworthy that the CL spectra showed some red shift compared to the maximum FL. This phenomenon could be explained by the lower energy spacings of the surface states of P/Cl-CDs (for CL emission) compared to the energy for the most intense FL [45]. The CL spectrum exhibited a small amplitude after 620 nm, which was consistent with the emission of the excited state of Mn(II) (Mn(II)*), so it was speculated that Mn(II)* also acted as an emitting species in the system [46]. Meanwhile, the characteristic absorption of KMnO4 at about 530 nm disappeared after mixing with the P/Cl-CDs solution, indicating that a reaction between P/Cl-CDs and KMnO4 occurred.

The production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) had attracted much attention because of its important role in mechanism research. To determine the involvement of free radicals in the CL reaction, the effect of some free radical probes, including ascorbic acid as a general free radical probe, thiourea as a probe of hydroxyl radical (•OH), NaN3 as a probe of singlet oxygen (1O2), and nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) as a probe of the superoxide radical (•O2 −) on the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system, was investigated [6,47]. As shown in Fig. 3d, more than 60% of the CL signal was quenched by the addition of 1.0 mM of these free radical probes, indicating the existence of these free radicals in the CL reaction.

Based on the above experiments and discussions, the following possible reaction mechanism was proposed. In the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4 reaction, P/Cl-CDs* were formed when P/Cl-CDs were oxidized by KMnO4, and then emitted CL by electron-hole recombination. KMnO4 as an oxidant could inject holes into the P/Cl-CDs. These holes then interacted with the electrons thermally excited into the high energy band of the P/Cl-CDs [48], resulting in electron-hole annihilation and CL emission at 535 nm. It was reported that the HSO3 − could be oxidized by KMnO4 to produce a •HSO3 radical, which recombined to form S2O6 2−. This ion disproportionated into a SO4 2− and a SO2*, which was the carrier of the very weak CL [49,50]. The energy of the SO2* may be match the smallest energy band gap of P/Cl-CDs [6]. The more chemical energy that matches the required excitation energy, the stronger the CL intensity will be and the higher efficiency could be achieved. In the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 CL system, there were •OH, •O2 −, 1O2 and •SO3 2− radical [6]. The •OH injected into P/Cl-CDs generated positively charged P/Cl-CDs•+, the •O2 − injected into the P/Cl-CDs generated negatively charged P/Cl-CDs•–, P/Cl-CDs•+ and P/Cl-CDs•– underwent electron transfer annihilation and combined to generate the P/Cl-CDs* and performed CL emission [51]. It is known that the dimol and monomol singlet-oxygen species are in equilibrium with one another [52], so the recombination of 1O2 can evolve dimol singlet-oxygen (1O2)2*. The generated (1O2)2* can cause light emission at the bands of 381, 477, and 578 nm [53], which overlapped the excitation wavelength of P/Cl-CDs. Hence, the energy from the (1O2)2* could transfer to P/Cl-CDs and generate P/Cl-CDs*, which returned to the ground-state and released photons [54–56]. The SO2* may also transfer its energy to P/Cl-CDs through energy transfer. In brief, P/Cl-CDs could accept the energy from (1O2)2* and SO2* to become P/Cl-CDs*, which returned to its ground state with the enhanced CL emission.

As is known to all, KMnO4 can oxidize I− to form iodine. In mixing solution of KMnO4 and I−, the blue appeared after adding starch, indicating the formation of I2 and the reaction occurrence between KMnO4 and I−. Therefore, the CL signal decreased when I− appeared in the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system.
All in all, based on the above experimental data and discussion results, a possible reaction mechanism was proposed as shown in Scheme 1 .

The batch CL experiments showed that I− could obviously inhibit the CL intensity of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system, and the response time was about 1.5 s after adding I− into this CL system (Fig. 3a). This fast response was suitable for FI analysis. For sensitive detection of I−, the influence of various conditions including the concentration of KMnO4, NaHSO3 and P/Cl-CDs on the CL signal were optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 a, b and c, the subsequent CL experiments were performed at 0.2 mM KMnO4, 4.0 mM NaHSO3 and 3.0% P/Cl-CDs.

Under the above mentioned optimal conditions, the analytical performances of the P/Cl-CDs-KMnO4-NaHSO3 system were investigated. As indicated in Fig. 4d and e, the CL intensity showed a gradual downward trend with increasing I− concentration. There was a good linear relationship between the CL response and the logarithm of I− concentration from 0.1 to 70.0 μΜ, according to the regression equation: ΔI = 231.9 + 464.7 lg[I] ([I] was the concentration of I−, μM), with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9955. The detection limit (3σ) for analyzing I− was 90.0 nM, The relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.7% for 11 determinations of 0.5 μM I−. The sample throughput was 120/h. Some figures of merit for the established method were compared with that of several methods reported for analysis of I−. The results were summarized in Table 1 , which indicated that the introduced method had a lower or comparable detection limit compared to most of previous methods. Moreover, this method extended the application of heteroatom doped carbon dots in CL systems as well as food analysis.

The selectivity of the CL method for I− was evaluated by monitoring the changes in CL intensity caused by the addition of potentially interfering foreign species. The influences of various extraneous species on the CL system in the presence of 0.5 μM I− were studied. The tolerable limit of a foreign species was assumed to be a relative error no more than ±5% in recovery at a concentration of 0.5 μM I−. The results were summarized in Fig. 5 , which showed that none of these species interfered with I− determination. Fig. 5 also confirmed the suitable selectivity of the method for analysis of real samples.

The method was used for the determination of iodide in edible salt samples. To examine the accuracy of developed method, real samples were spiked with suitable amounts of the I− standard solution and the results were shown in Table 2 . It was found that good recoveries were obtained for the measured samples. Additionally, the results obtained with the proposed method were in good agreement with those obtained by chloroform extraction colorimetry, a classical method for the determination of iodine content in food. Student's t-test confirmed the accuracy of the results (Table 2). Therefore, the method was suitable for the detection of iodide in the salt samples.

In summary, a CL phenomenon was described from the oxidation reaction between P/Cl-CDs and KMnO4, which was enhanced by the addition of NaHSO3. First, P/Cl-CDs were used to promote KMnO4 to inject holes into P/Cl-CDs to produce P/Cl-CDs* accompanied by light emission. Subsequently, energy transfer occurred from (1O2)2* and SO2* to P/Cl-CDs, and more P/Cl-CDs* were formed, resulting in greatly enhanced CL emission. Using this particular signal amplification strategy, a highly sensitive and selective CDs-based CL for I− detection was constructed based on inhibition by the competitive consumption of KMnO4. This proposed method was successfully applied to the real sample and gave effective and satisfactory results.

Yamei Jiang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaowei Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization, Investigation. Suqin Han: Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (2013011013–3), and Fundamental Research Fund of Modern College of Humanities and Sciences of Shanxi Normal University (2020JCYJ01).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies in liquid-phase chemiluminescence and their applications in bioassay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Su Y, Deng D, Zhang L, Song H, Lv Y. Strategies in liquid-phase chemiluminescence and their applicationsin bioassay. Trends Anal Chem 2016;2:394-411. http://doi:10.1016/j.trac.2016.07.002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of periodate-hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescence by nitrogen doped carbon dots and its application for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shah SNA, Li HF, Lin JM. Enhancement of periodate-hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescence by nitrogen doped carbon dots and its application for the determination of pyrogallol and gallic acid. Talanta 2016;153:23−30. http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2016.02.056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Radical-triggered chemiluminescence of phenanthroline derivatives: an insight into radical-aromatic interaction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shah SNA, Shah AH, Dou XN, Khan M, Lin L, Lin JM. Radical-triggered chemiluminescence of phenanthroline derivatives: an insight into radical-aromatic interaction. ACS Omega 2019;4:15004−15011. http://doi:10.1021/acsomega.9b01785.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A prolegomena of periodate and peroxide chemiluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shah SNA, Khan M, Rehman ZU. A prolegomena of periodate and peroxide chemiluminescence. Trend Anal Chem 2020;122:115722. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trac.2019.115722.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Redox cycling of iron by carbon dot enhanced chemiluminescence: mechanism of electron-hole induction in carbon dot

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shah SNA, Lin L, Zheng YZ, Zhang DK, Lin JM. Redox cycling of iron by carbon dot enhanced chemiluminescence: mechanism of electron-hole induction in carbon dot. Phys Chem Chem Phys 2017;19:21604. http://doi:10.1039/c7cp03724d.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemiluminescence character of ZnS quantum dots with bisulphite-hydrogen peroxide system in acidic medium

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shah SNA, Zheng YZ, Li HF, Lin JM. Chemiluminescence character of ZnS quantum dots with bisulphite-hydrogen peroxide system in acidic medium. J. Phys. Chem. C 2016;120:9308−9316. http://doi:10.1021/acs.jpcc.6b01925.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Radical pair-driven luminescence of quantum dots for specific detection of peroxynitrite in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou WJ, Cao YQ, Sui DD, Lu C. Radical pair-driven luminescence of quantum dots for specific detection of peroxynitrite in living cells. Anal Chem 2016;88:2659-2665. http://doi:10.1021/acs.analchem.5b03827.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cerium doped magnetite nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of metronidazole via chemiluminescence assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Orooji Y, Irani-nezhad MH, Hassandoost R, Khataee A, Pouran SR, Joo SW. Cerium doped magnetite nanoparticles for highly sensitive detection of metronidazole via chemiluminescence assay. Spectrochim Acta A 2020; 234: 118272. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118272.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A chemiluminescent method for the detection of H2O2 and glucose based on intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of WS2 quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Irani-nezhad MH, Khataee A, Hassanzadeh J, Orooji J. A chemiluminescent method for the detection of H2O2 and glucose based on intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of WS2 quantum dots. Molecules 2019;24:689. http://doi:10.3390/molecules24040689.
